reason: The text depicts Pharaoh and his servants uniting against the Israelites to recapture them, while Moses and the Israelites do not collaborate in the solution. Instead, the people express fear and complaint to Moses, asking why he brought them out to die in the wilderness. Moses does not engage in joint planning with the people but rather commands them to stand still and watch God's salvation. The actual deliverance is accomplished by God alone—Moses raises his staff by divine command, God parts the sea and hardens Pharaoh's heart, and the people remain passive, simply walking through on dry ground. The 'collaboration' only appears at the very end in the form of faith in God and belief in Moses after the crisis has been resolved by divine action, not through any joint effort 'for the betterment of the community.' The passage portrays divine salvation, not collaborative leadership.
